---
title: ""
author: ""
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: lualatex
    toc: false
    number-sections: true
fontsize: 12pt
geometry: margin=1in
header-includes:
  - \usepackage{hyperref}
  - \hypersetup{
      colorlinks=true,
      linkcolor=black,
      urlcolor=black,
      citecolor=black
    }
---

\begin{center}
{\LARGE \textbf{Personal Knowledge Graph Memory Engine and Career Development Assistant}}\\[0.5em]
{\large Turning conversations into structured long-term memory}\\[0.2em]
{\large for personalized and evolving career guidance}\\[0.8em]
{\large Jiayi Peng, Kexin Lyu, Yiran Tao, Chenxi Guo}
\end{center}
\vspace{1em}

\hypersetup{linkcolor=black}
\tableofcontents
\newpage


# Abstract {.unnumbered}

We present an Orchestrator-Driven Personal Knowledge Graph Memory Engine and Career Development Assistant that transforms conversational interactions into structured, queryable knowledge representations. Our system marks a significant transition from a static, multi-engine retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline to an autonomous agentic architecture managed by a central Orchestrator Agent. This orchestrator leverages a Groq-hosted Large Language Model (LLM) to perform real-time assessment of conversational state, generate reasoning traces for transparency, and execute strategic activation of downstream recommendation agents. The system automatically extracts semantically meaningful career signals—including goals, professional preferences, active projects, and technical toolsets—and organizes them within a temporally-aware, graph-based memory architecture using NetworkX. By integrating information extraction with fuzzy entity linking, we ensure that professional concepts are accurately merged across multi-turn dialogues, preserving relational context that is often lost in flat retrieval mechanisms. Our evaluation framework utilizes a three-layered approach: technical metrics (achieving an Entities F1 of 0.88 and Relations F1 of 0.97), qualitative AI-as-a-Judge scoring for faithfulness and utility, and narrative-based profile validation. The results demonstrate that the orchestrator-driven approach significantly reduces redundant processing and improves the groundedness of career guidance by mapping user profiles to the O*NET occupational taxonomy. This work contributes a principled foundation for autonomous agents with persistent, structured, and interpretable long-term memory, addressing the critical "context-window" limitation of modern LLMs in the domain of professional growth and career counseling. Our findings suggest that agentic orchestration is essential for bridging the gap between disconnected processing engines and a cohesive, personalized user experience.

In the realm of artificial intelligence, particularly in conversational agents and personal assistants, the ability to retain and utilize long-term memory is paramount for delivering personalized and context-aware interactions. Traditional approaches often struggle with the dynamic and relational nature of human conversation, relying on limited context windows or flat retrieval methods that fail to capture the intricate connections between pieces of information. Our project addresses this challenge by introducing the Personal Knowledge Graph Memory Engine and Career Development Assistant, an orchestrator-driven system designed to transform unstructured conversational data into a structured, queryable knowledge graph for professional guidance. By centralizing control within an autonomous Orchestrator Agent, we ensure that memory extraction and career recommendations are executed with transparent reasoning and strategic activation.

## Research Questions
Our work is driven by three primary research questions:
1. **Structural Fidelity**: Can a graph-based memory architecture more accurately represent the relational complexity of a user's professional journey compared to flat context windows?
2. **Agentic Orchestration**: How can a central orchestrator improve the efficiency and utility of a multi-agent career counseling system by conditionally activating domain-specific engines?
3. **Implicit Signal Inference**: To what extent can an LLM-driven extraction layer surface latent professional signals (e.g., work styles, soft skills) from unstructured technical dialogue?


# Related Work

## Knowledge Graphs and Relational Memory

Knowledge graphs have long been recognized for their ability to represent complex relationships between entities, offering a structured approach to information storage and retrieval that surpasses traditional databases in semantic richness. Seminal work by Bordes et al. (2013) introduced TransE, a foundational embedding model that represents entities and relations in a shared vector space, enabling scalable inference over large knowledge bases. More recently, Pan et al. (2024) provided a comprehensive survey of integrating LLMs with knowledge graphs, demonstrating that hybrid architectures achieve superior performance on knowledge-intensive tasks compared to either approach in isolation. Our system builds on this tradition by using NetworkX MultiDiGraphs to represent user-specific relational facts extracted from conversation, with typed nodes (Tool, Skill, Project, Goal) and temporally weighted edges.

## Persistent and Long-Term Memory in Conversational Agents

A central limitation of current LLM-based assistants is that context is discarded at the end of each session. Packer et al. (2023) introduced MemGPT, which addresses this by treating the LLM's finite context window as a virtual operating system with explicit memory management: facts are moved between in-context and external storage based on relevance. Wang et al. (2023) proposed LongMem, a retrieval-augmented framework that enables LLMs to recall arbitrary-length conversation history via a side-memory module. Our work shares the objective of persistent, cross-session memory but focuses specifically on \textbf{career-domain} signals and organizes them in a typed knowledge graph rather than a flat vector store, enabling structured queries such as "what tools has this user actively used?" or "which career goals have been mentioned more than once?".

## Information Extraction and Named Entity Recognition

Populating knowledge graphs from raw conversation requires robust information extraction. Honnibal \& Montani (2017) demonstrated with spaCy that rule-augmented neural pipelines achieve strong NER performance with minimal labeling overhead, a design choice mirrored in our hybrid LLM-plus-rule extraction architecture. Devlin et al. (2019) showed that BERT-based fine-tuning achieves state-of-the-art results across a range of NER benchmarks, establishing the paradigm of transformer-based extraction that we leverage through Groq-hosted inference. Our extractor follows a dual-path strategy: an LLM-based path (llama-3.3-70b-versatile) for semantic understanding and a regex/rule-based fallback for reliability, with entity linking using fuzzy matching to merge surface-form variants into canonical graph nodes.

## Career Counseling and O\*NET-Aligned Guidance

Automated career guidance systems have increasingly relied on occupational taxonomies for structured recommendation. The O\*NET database (U.S. Department of Labor, 2024) provides a rich 923-occupation taxonomy covering Knowledge, Skills, Abilities, Work Styles, and Interests, making it a natural target for skill-gap analysis. Zheng et al. (2023) introduced LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation, which we adopt to score our system across Completeness, Faithfulness, and Career Utility dimensions, providing a scalable qualitative assessment beyond precision/recall alone.

# System Architecture

## Orchestrator Agent

We introduce `OrchestratorAgent` (~80 LOC) that makes per‑turn decisions using a Groq‑hosted LLM. If the Groq service is unavailable, it falls back to a deterministic rule‑based policy. The orchestrator determines whether to activate the `RecommendationAgent` (`run\_recommender` flag) and generates a `reasoning\_trace` describing its decision process.

### Session State Updates

The session schema now includes two additional fields:
- `orchestrator`: object containing the latest `reasoning` (string) and `run\_recommender` (boolean).
- `reasoning\_trace`: list of strings documenting the step‑by‑step reasoning for debugging and auditability.

### /api/chat Flow Adjustments

1. **Orchestrator Decision** – The orchestrator runs first, setting `run\_recommender` and producing `orchestrator.reasoning`.
2. **Memory & Career Agents** – These agents always execute to update the knowledge graph.
3. **Recommendation Agent** – Executes conditionally only if `run\_recommender` is true and sufficient signals have been collected.

### Updated Response Payload

The API response now includes:
- `orchestrator.reasoning`: the textual reasoning from the orchestrator.
- `orchestrator.run\_recommender`: boolean flag indicating whether recommendations were generated.
- Existing fields (`memory`, `career`, `recommendations`, etc.) remain unchanged.

## Core Components and Responsibilities

The Personal Knowledge Graph Memory Engine and Career Development Assistant is designed as a modular, multi-layered system that processes conversational data into a structured knowledge graph and enables intelligent retrieval.

## Overall Data Flow

The system operates as a sophisticated multi-stage pipeline, beginning with the ingestion of raw conversational logs and culminating in the generation of personalized career guidance. The data flow is structured into four primary logical phases:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Ingestion and Normalization}: Raw dialogue data is segmented into individual speaker turns. Each turn is timestamped and assigned a unique identifier to maintain temporal and conversational context throughout the pipeline.
    \item \textbf{Decomposed Extraction}: The system utilizes a specialized extraction agent to identify professional signals from each human turn. This process is not a simple keyword search; instead, the LLM analyzes the text to extract complex entities and their relational roles, such as associating a specific tool (e.g., Python) with a project context or a behavioral trait with a career goal.
    \item \textbf{Graph Synthesis}: Extracted signals are passed to the graph memory layer where entity linking is performed. Using a combination of fuzzy matching and type-aware constraints, the system merges new extractions into existing nodes to prevent duplication while updating temporal metadata like 'last seen' timestamps. This results in a consolidated, evolving knowledge representation of the user.
    \item \textbf{Recommendation Mapping}: At the final stage, the user's career profile—represented as a subgraph of relevant skills, knowledge, and goals—is projected onto the O\*NET professional taxonomy. The system calculates a similarity score across multiple vectors and identifies educational or experiential gaps, which are then used to construct the final response.
\end{enumerate}

## Input/Data Layer

- **Components:** `data\_pipeline.py`
- **Functionality:**
  - Data loading
  - Turn segmentation
  - Noise filtering
  - Normalization
  - Sampling
- **Output:** JSONL conversational turns

## Extraction \& Linking Layer

- **Components:** `extraction\_agent.py`, `graph\_memory.py`
- **Functionality:**
  - LLM-based extraction
  - Rule-based fallback
  - Entity linking
  - Data cleaning
- **Output:** structured memory + graph updates

## Cognitive Inference Layer

A critical challenge in conversational career counseling is that users rarely explicitly claim soft skills or cognitive traits (e.g., "Critical Thinking"). Our system addresses this by implementing an \textbf{Implicit Signal Inference Layer}. This layer analyzes the frequency of technical tool usage, the complexity of active projects, and multi-turn behavioral patterns to autonomously inject inferred attributes into the user profile. This ensures that the resulting knowledge representation is not just a collection of stated facts but a holistic professional persona.

## Graph Memory Layer

- **Components:** `graph\_memory.py` (NetworkX)
- **Functionality:**
  - Graph storage (MultiDiGraph)
  - Node and edge attributes
  - Export capability
- **Output:** `graph\_memory.json`

## Two-Stage Recommendation Engine

The core of the professional guidance system is a multi-stage recommendation and reasoning engine that transitions from broad O\*NET mapping to deep, personalized career re-ranking.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Stage 1: Vector-Based Shortlisting (Common Semantic Space)}: Both the user's graph-based profile and the O\*NET occupations are mapped into a shared, high-dimensional space. Using the weighted cosine similarity of Knowledge, Skills, and Work Styles, the system generates a \textbf{shortlist of the Top-20 candidates}. This stage ensures high efficiency and mathematical stability across the 923-occupation search space.
    \item \textbf{Stage 2: LLM-Driven Re-ranking (Contextual Understanding)}: While Stage 1 ensures structural alignment, it lacks semantic depth. Stage 2 utilizes a \textit{llama-3.3-70b-versatile} model (Dubey et al., 2024) via the Groq inference engine to incorporate the \textbf{full conversational context}. This allows the engine to distinguish between technical capability and deeper professional intent (e.g., distinguishing between a user who \textit{knows} Python and one who \textit{intends to apply} it in financial contexts), producing a prioritized \textbf{Top-5 list} with reasoning.
    \item \textbf{Stage 3: Decision Support \& Gap Analysis}: The system identifies missing skills and knowledge to provide actionable feedback. For example, a user with a strong \textbf{Computer Science (CS)} profile who expresses interest in high-frequency trading would be guided toward a \textbf{Quantitative Analyst (Quant)} path by identifying specific gaps in \textit{Mathematics, Statistics,} and \textit{Finance}.
\end{itemize}

## Key Design Choices and Trade-offs

- **Hybrid Extraction**: LLM-based understanding with rule-based reliability fallback.
- **Hierarchical Retrieval**: Combining structural vector similarity (Stage 1) with semantic LLM re-ranking (Stage 2).
- **Type-Aware Mapping**: Constraining O\*NET mappings by node categories (Tools, Skills, etc.) to minimize noise.
- **Temporal Weighting**: Using recency and frequency decay to ensure that currently active projects and goals carry higher weight in the user's profile.

## Evolution to Agentic Behavior

A pivotal development in our project was the transition from a traditional multi-engine pipeline to a unified **agentic architecture**. Initially, the memory and career engines functioned as independent, sequential modules with limited cross-communication. This "passive pipeline" approach presented several **unexpected challenges**:
- **Redundant Processing**: The system would attempt recommendations even when no new career signals were present, leading to generic or repetitive output.
- **Opacity**: It was difficult to trace why the system made specific extraction choices across multiple turns.
- **Lack of Autonomy**: The engines did not exhibit the self-correcting or decision-making behavior characteristic of true AI agents.

To address these, we integrated these components under the control of an **Orchestrator Agent**. This integration transforms the system from a passive processor into an active participant. The orchestrator assesses the state of the conversation, evaluates the density of signals collected, and determines whether the `RecommendationAgent` has sufficient information to provide a meaningful response. This "thinking before acting" phase (documented in the `reasoning\_trace`) is the hallmark of agentic behavior, ensuring that the system only intervenes when it can provide high-utility guidance.



# Evaluation

## Evaluation Methodology

To ensure architectural consistency and delivery quality, we adopted a hierarchical evaluation framework consisting of three distinct layers. This approach verifies that the system is not only structurally accurate but also semantically coherent and professionally useful.

\subsubsection{Layer 1: Technical Metrics (Recall/Precision/F1)}
This foundational layer measures the system's objective extraction accuracy. We performed a direct comparison against a \textbf{manually curated gold-standard dataset} of 22 dialogue turns drawn from the ShareGPT Vicuna Unfiltered dataset (\texttt{gold\_extraction\_enriched.json}). Each turn was hand-labeled with entity spans and relation triples (source, target, relation\_type) and then manually reviewed and enriched by the team to ensure quality. The extractor was evaluated using the \textit{llama-3.1-8b-instant} model via Groq. We specifically focused on \textbf{Relation-level F1 scores} to assess the engine's ability to maintain relational context between skills and projects, using a fuzzy-match threshold of 0.5 (token overlap or SequenceMatcher ratio).

\subsubsection{Layer 2: AI-as-a-Judge (Qualitative Scoring)}
Building upon objective metrics, we employed GPT-4o as an automated judge (Zheng et al., 2023) across two independent evaluation sets, each targeting a different system component:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Memory Engine Sub-evaluation} (16 turns, ShareGPT gold set): The same 22-turn ShareGPT gold set used for Layer 1 was re-evaluated qualitatively. GPT-4o scored each turn's extraction on Completeness, Faithfulness, and Utility relative to the original user message. 16 of the 22 turns produced non-empty extractions and were therefore scored.
    \item \textbf{Career Pipeline Sub-evaluation} (30 dialogues, purpose-built career dataset): 30 complete multi-turn dialogues were randomly sampled (fixed seed = 0) from the full 380-dialogue purpose-built career dataset (\texttt{career\_dialogues\_380.json}, IDs \texttt{career\_dev\_001} through \texttt{career\_dev\_380}). GPT-4o scored each dialogue's aggregated career profile across Completeness, Faithfulness, and Career Utility dimensions. This dataset is entirely separate from the ShareGPT gold set used in the memory engine evaluation.
\end{itemize}

This two-track design allows us to separately assess extraction accuracy at the turn level (memory engine) and end-to-end profile quality at the dialogue level (career pipeline).

\subsubsection{Layer 3: Narrative-based Profile Validation}
To bridge the gap between structured data and human-readable guidance, the system generates a \textbf{"Career Profile Narrative"} for each user. This layer validates the agent's holistic understanding of the user's intent by synthesizing extracted nodes into a cohesive professional summary, ensuring the inferred persona matches the conversational source.

# Preliminary Results

## Technical Validation (Layer 1)

Quantitative evaluation against gold-standard labels demonstrated high structural fidelity. The memory engine achieved an **Entities F1 score of 0.88** and a **Relations F1 score of 0.97**, indicating that the relational structure of the user's professional journey is preserved with exceptional accuracy. Furthermore, both **Noise Robustness** and **Self-Consistency** achieved scores of \textbf{1.0}, confirming the stability of the entity linking and merger logic under conversational variance.

## LLM-based Performance (Layer 2)

### Memory Engine Sub-evaluation (16 gold turns, GPT-4o judge)

At the extraction level, the memory engine performed strongly:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Faithfulness}: 4.31 / 5.0 — High grounding with minimal hallucination at the turn level.
    \item \textbf{Utility}: 3.94 / 5.0 — Extracted facts are largely actionable for downstream profiling.
    \item \textbf{Completeness}: 3.50 / 5.0 — The system captures most explicit signals per turn, but misses implicit context.
\end{itemize}

### Career Pipeline Sub-evaluation (30 career dialogues, GPT-4o judge)

When evaluated end-to-end at the full-dialogue level, scores are more conservative, reflecting the compounding difficulty of aggregating signals across multiple turns:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Faithfulness}: 3.30 / 5.0 — Some hallucinated items appear during profile aggregation, where the system infers attributes not directly grounded in any individual turn.
    \item \textbf{Career Utility}: 3.13 / 5.0 — Profiles are useful but lack specificity in development area suggestions.
    \item \textbf{Completeness}: 3.13 / 5.0 — The weakest metric, indicating systematic gaps in capturing the full breadth of career signals across a dialogue.
\end{itemize}

The gap between the turn-level Completeness (3.50) and the dialogue-level Completeness (3.13) indicates that signal loss compounds across turns: information captured correctly in isolation is not always successfully aggregated into the final career profile.

### Analysis of Completeness Gaps

A review of per-turn and per-dialogue judge comments identifies two primary failure modes:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Implicit and Latent Career Signals}: The extractor consistently surfaces explicit entities (e.g., "Python", "FastAPI") but misses inferred behavioral or contextual signals. For example, a user describing repeated iteration over a high-frequency trading model implicitly signals quantitative aptitude and risk-tolerance — signals that require multi-turn reasoning rather than single-turn pattern matching. The judge flagged recurring gaps in "portfolio optimization" interest and "fast-paced work style" even when these were evident from the conversational arc.

    \item \textbf{Multi-Entity Tool Contexts}: When a user describes a complex technical environment — e.g., "connecting GraphQL-engine to a Digital Ocean PostgreSQL database on a Linux server" — the extractor captured only \textit{postgresql} and omitted \textit{GraphQL-engine}, \textit{Digital Ocean}, and \textit{Linux server}. This pattern recurs across technical setup messages, suggesting the relevance filter (which gates on first-person signals) over-prunes informationally dense third-person technical descriptions.
\end{enumerate}

These failure modes share a common root cause: the current architecture processes each turn in isolation without a cross-turn inference pass. Addressing this is a primary target for future work.

## Narrative and Guidance Utility (Layer 3)

The generation of structured Career Profile Narratives enabled a successful mapping to the O\*NET 28.2 taxonomy. In a representative test case, the system autonomously identified \textbf{Web Developers} as the primary career match (Similarity Score: \textbf{0.7329}). The narrative-based synthesis allowed the engine to identify high-priority knowledge gaps in areas such as \textit{Communications and Media}, providing a practical roadmap for the user's next steps. This confirms that the graph-based memory is not merely a collection of nodes but a coherent representation of a user's professional trajectory.

# Models and Technologies

The system leverages a specialized stack designed for high-speed inference and structured knowledge management:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Large Language Models}: We utilize the \textit{Llama 3} family of models, specifically \textit{llama-3.3-70b-versatile} for high-reasoning tasks (orchestration and re-ranking) and \textit{llama-3.1-8b-instant} for high-throughput extraction. These models provide a balance between semantic depth and operational speed.
    \item \textbf{Inference Infrastructure}: All LLM calls are routed through the \textbf{Groq LPU™ Inference Engine}, which provides the sub-second latency required for interactive conversational applications.
    \item \textbf{Graph Framework}: \textbf{NetworkX} is used for the underlying MultiDiGraph representation, providing a flexible schema for typed professional entities and temporal relations.
    \item \textbf{Taxonomy Alignment}: The \textbf{O*NET 28.2 Database} serves as the ground-truth ontology for occupational mapping, ensuring that all recommendations align with standard labor market classifications.
    \item \textbf{Embedding Models}: We use dense vector embeddings for the Stage 1 retrieval, enabling efficient similarity search across the 923-occupation space.
\end{itemize}


# Responsible AI Considerations

The development of a career-focused agent necessitates a rigorous approach to ethics and safety. Our system incorporates several measures to ensure responsible AI behavior:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Bias and Fairness}: Occupational recommendations can inadvertently reflect historical biases present in training data. We use the O*NET taxonomy as a grounded, authoritative baseline to minimize ad-hoc stereotypes. Additionally, our two-stage recommendation engine uses Stage 1 (Vector) for objective matching and Stage 2 (LLM) for contextual nuance, providing a system of checks and balances.
    \item \textbf{Hallucination Mitigation}: By grounding recommendations in a structured knowledge graph (the "Source of Truth") rather than relying solely on the LLM's internal weights, we significantly reduce the risk of fictitious career paths or skill requirements.
    \item \textbf{Privacy and PII}: While the current demo focuses on technical signals, future iterations will include automated PII (Personally Identifiable Information) scrubbing before data is sent to the graph memory layer, ensuring that professional guidance does not compromise user privacy.
    \item \textbf{Transparency}: The introduction of the \texttt{reasoning\_trace} and \texttt{orchestrator.reasoning} fields provides a transparent audit trail of why the system made specific decisions, allowing users to understand the logic behind their career guidance.
\end{itemize}


# Conclusion and Future Work

## Summary of Contributions

We have developed an Orchestrator-Driven Personal Knowledge Graph Memory Engine and Career Development Assistant that bridges the gap between unstructured conversation and structured O\*NET-aligned professional guidance. Our core contribution is the implementation of an autonomous agentic architecture where a central orchestrator manages domain-specific engines, ensuring transparent reasoning and strategic recommendation activation. Our system demonstrates that graph-based relational memory, when coupled with agentic orchestration, can capture and utilize evolving career signals more effectively than traditional flat context windows or passive retrieval pipelines.

## Future Directions

- \textbf{Improving Implicit Signal Inference}: The dialogue-level Completeness score of 3.13/5.0 (career pipeline) reveals a systematic gap in capturing latent career signals — behavioral patterns, inferred cognitive strengths, and tool-environment context that are implied across multiple turns rather than explicitly stated in any single turn. Future work will introduce a dedicated \textit{cross-turn inference pass} that aggregates per-turn extractions into a running profile and uses the LLM to infer implicit traits (e.g., "quantitative aptitude" from repeated references to statistical modeling tools). This directly addresses the primary bottleneck identified in our evaluation.

- \textbf{Dynamic Embeddings}: Incorporating temporally-aware or task-specific embedding models (e.g., fine-tuned Sentence-BERT variants) for more precise O\*NET concept mapping, particularly in emerging technical domains not well-represented in static embedding spaces.

- \textbf{Visualization}: Developing interactive graph visualization tools for user self-exploration, enabling users to inspect, confirm, or correct their inferred knowledge graph—supporting a human-in-the-loop feedback loop.

- \textbf{Human-in-the-loop}: Adding mechanisms for users to manually edit or verify their graph nodes, which would simultaneously improve personalization and generate a curated dataset for future fine-tuning.

# References {.unnumbered}

\begin{itemize}
    \item Bordes, A., Usunier, N., Garcia-Durán, A., Weston, J., \& Yakhnenko, O. (2013). Translating embeddings for modeling multi-relational data. \textit{Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS)}, 26.

    \item Devlin, J., Chang, M.-W., Lee, K., \& Toutanova, K. (2019). BERT: Pre-training of deep bidirectional transformers for language understanding. In \textit{Proceedings of NAACL-HLT 2019} (pp. 4171--4186). Association for Computational Linguistics.

    \item Dubey, A., Jauhri, A., Pandey, A., Kadian, A., Al-Dahle, A., Letman, A., et al. (2024). The Llama 3 herd of models. arXiv:2407.21783.

    \item Groq Cloud. (2024). \textit{LPU™ Inference Engine Documentation}. Retrieved from https://groq.com/

    \item Hagberg, A., Schult, D., \& Swart, P. (2008). Exploring network structure, dynamics, and function using NetworkX. In \textit{Proceedings of the 7th Python in Science Conference (SciPy 2008)} (pp. 11--15).

    \item Honnibal, M., \& Montani, I. (2017). spaCy 2: Natural language understanding with Bloom embeddings, convolutional neural networks and incremental parsing. \textit{Unpublished technical report}.

    \item O\*NET Resource Center. (2024). \textit{The O\*NET 28.2 Database}. Retrieved from https://www.onetcenter.org/

    \item Packer, C., Fang, V., Patil, S. G., Lin, K., Wooders, S., \& Gonzalez, J. E. (2023). MemGPT: Towards LLMs as operating systems. arXiv:2310.08560.

    \item Pan, S., Luo, L., Wang, Y., Chen, C., Wang, J., \& Wu, X. (2024). Unifying large language models and knowledge graphs: A roadmap. \textit{IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering}, 36(7), 3580--3599.

    \item Reimers, N., \& Gurevych, I. (2019). Sentence-BERT: Sentence embeddings using Siamese BERT-networks. In \textit{Proceedings of EMNLP 2019} (pp. 3982--3992). Association for Computational Linguistics.

    \item ShareGPT Dataset. (2023). \textit{Vicuna Unfiltered Cleaned Dataset}. Retrieved from https://huggingface.co/datasets/anon8231489123/ShareGPT\_Vicuna\_unfiltered/

    \item Wang, W., Dong, L., Cheng, H., Liu, X., Yan, X., Gao, J., \& Wei, F. (2023). Augmenting language models with long-term memory. arXiv:2306.07174.

    \item Zheng, L., Chiang, W. L., Sheng, Y., Zhuang, S., Wu, Z., Zhuang, Y., ... \& Stoica, I. (2023). Judging LLM-as-a-judge with MT-bench and chatbot arena. \textit{Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems}, 36.
\end{itemize}
